title: Linear Algebra & Matrix Decompositions
learning_outcomes: Define vector spaces, bases, linear independence, spans, and orthogonal projections from first principles.
Derive the orthogonal projection matrix formulation and explain its relation to the normal equations in OLS regression.
Understand the Spectral Theorem and perform Eigendecomposition on square symmetric matrices.
Derive the Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) of any real rectangular matrix and explain the geometric interpretation of its components.
Apply the Eckart-Young-Mirsky Theorem to construct optimal low-rank matrix approximations for applications in collaborative filtering and Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA).

content_sections.title: The Geometry of Vector Spaces and Orthogonal Projections
content_sections.content:
Linear algebra represents the fundamental language of machine learning. Rather than viewing data as isolated tables of numbers, we model datasets as coordinate spaces where algebraic properties dictate the behavior of our algorithms. A vector space $\mathcal{V}$ over the field of real numbers $\mathbb{R}$ is a collection of elements (vectors) closed under vector addition and scalar multiplication. More formally, any linear combination of vectors in the space must itself remain within the space. A spanning set of $\mathcal{V}$ is a collection of vectors whose linear combinations can cover the entire space, whereas a **basis** is a minimal spanning set of linearly independent vectors. The cardinality of the basis defines the dimension of the vector space.

In practical machine learning, we often encounter datasets where the target labels vector $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^m$ does not lie within the subspace spanned by the columns of our feature matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ (where $m > n$ and $\mathbf{X}$ has full column rank). Because $\mathbf{y}$ is outside the column space of $\mathbf{X}$, the linear system $\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{y}$ has no exact solution. To find the optimal parameter vector $\boldsymbol{\beta}$, we must project $\mathbf{y}$ orthogonally onto the column space of $\mathbf{X}$. Let $\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}$ be this projection. The residual vector $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{p}$ represents the error between our projection and the true target. For $\mathbf{p}$ to be the closest point to $\mathbf{y}$ in the column space of $\mathbf{X}$, the residual $\mathbf{r}$ must be orthogonal to the column space of $\mathbf{X}$. Since the columns of $\mathbf{X}$ form a basis for this space, the dot product of any column of $\mathbf{X}$ with the residual must equal zero. In matrix notation, this orthogonality condition is expressed as the normal equations:

$$\mathbf{X}^{\top}(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \mathbf{0}$$

Expanding this equation yields:

$$\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{0} \implies \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y}$$

Because the columns of $\mathbf{X}$ are linearly independent, the square matrix $\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}$ is positive definite and thus invertible. We solve for $\boldsymbol{\beta}$:

$$\boldsymbol{\beta} = (\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y}$$

Substituting this back into the expression for our projected vector $\mathbf{p}$, we obtain:

$$\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y}$$

The matrix $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\top}$ is the orthogonal projection matrix. It maps any vector $\mathbf{y}$ in the ambient space onto the closest point in the column space of $\mathbf{X}$.

callouts.title: Vector Space Boundaries and the Closure Rule
callouts.content: Many beginners believe that any set of vectors can form a vector space. A common interview trap is asking whether the set of all vectors in $\mathbb{R}^2$ whose elements are non-negative forms a vector space. While this set is closed under addition, it is not closed under scalar multiplication by negative numbers (e.g., multiplying $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}^{\top}$ by $-1$ yields $\begin{bmatrix} -1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}^{\top}$, which lies outside the non-negative quadrant). Therefore, it violates the vector space axioms.

callouts.title: Numerical Instability of Normal Equations
callouts.content: Although the closed-form equation $\boldsymbol{\beta} = (\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{y}$ is mathematically clean, computing $(\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X})^{-1}$ directly in floating-point arithmetic is highly unstable when features are highly correlated (collinearity). The condition number of $\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}$ is the square of the condition number of $\mathbf{X}$. In production frameworks (like scikit-learn), engineers solve the system using QR decomposition or Singular Value Decomposition (via `pinv`) rather than directly inverting the normal equations matrix.

content_sections.title: Spectral Theory and Eigendecomposition of Symmetric Matrices
content_sections.content:
Square matrices represent transformations that scale and rotate vectors. Eigendecomposition breaks down a matrix into its invariant directions (eigenvectors) and their corresponding scaling factors (eigenvalues). For a square matrix $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, a non-zero vector $\mathbf{v}$ is an eigenvector of $\mathbf{A}$ if:

$$\mathbf{A}\mathbf{v} = \lambda\mathbf{v}$$

where $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$ is the eigenvalue. We solve this by finding the roots of the characteristic polynomial $\det(\mathbf{A} - \lambda\mathbf{I}) = 0$. If $\mathbf{A}$ has $n$ linearly independent eigenvectors, it can be diagonalized as $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{V}\mathbf{\Lambda}\mathbf{V}^{-1}$, where the columns of $\mathbf{V}$ are the eigenvectors and the diagonal matrix $\mathbf{\Lambda}$ contains the eigenvalues.

Symmetric matrices (where $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{A}^{\top}$) play a privileged role in machine learning. The Spectral Theorem guarantees that any real symmetric matrix $\mathbf{A}$ can be orthogonally diagonalized. This means its eigenvectors are mutually perpendicular (orthogonal) and can be normalized to have unit length, forming an orthogonal matrix $\mathbf{Q}$ (where $\mathbf{Q}^{\top}\mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{I}$). Thus, the decomposition simplifies to:

$$\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{\Lambda}\mathbf{Q}^{\top} = \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i \mathbf{q}_i \mathbf{q}_i^{\top}$$

This spectral decomposition expresses $\mathbf{A}$ as a weighted sum of rank-one orthogonal projection matrices ($\mathbf{q}_i \mathbf{q}_i^{\top}$), scaled by their eigenvalues. If we apply this to a covariance matrix, the eigenvalues represent the variance along each eigenvector direction, while the eigenvectors define the principal components.

callouts.title: Complex Eigenvalues and Non-Symmetric Matrices
callouts.content: Do not assume that all square matrices have real eigenvalues. Non-symmetric matrices can yield complex eigenvalues, which represent rotational behavior in the vector space. The guarantee of real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors applies strictly to real symmetric matrices. Always verify symmetry before applying spectral methods.

content_sections.title: Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)
content_sections.content:
Eigendecomposition is limited to square, diagonalizable matrices. Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) generalizes factorization to any rectangular matrix $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$. SVD factorizes $\mathbf{A}$ into three matrices:

$$\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{\top}$$

Where:
1. $\mathbf{U}$ is an $m \times m$ orthogonal matrix whose columns are the left-singular vectors of $\mathbf{A}$. These represent the orthonormal eigenvectors of the square symmetric matrix $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}^{\top}$.
2. $\mathbf{V}$ is an $n \times n$ orthogonal matrix whose columns are the right-singular vectors of $\mathbf{A}$. These represent the orthonormal eigenvectors of the square symmetric matrix $\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{A}$.
3. $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ is an $m \times n$ diagonal matrix containing non-negative, real singular values ($\sigma_i$) arranged in descending order. These singular values represent the square roots of the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{A}$.

Geometrically, SVD reveals that any linear transformation can be decomposed into an input space rotation ($\mathbf{V}^{\top}$), a stretching along coordinate axes ($\mathbf{\Sigma}$), and an output space rotation ($\mathbf{U}$). To derive SVD, we start with the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{A}$. Since $\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{A}$ is positive semi-definite, its eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ are non-negative. We define the singular values as $\sigma_i = \sqrt{\lambda_i}$. The right-singular vectors $\mathbf{v}_i$ are the orthonormal eigenvectors of $\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{A}$. For non-zero singular values, we define the left-singular vectors as $\mathbf{u}_i = \frac{1}{\sigma_i} \mathbf{A} \mathbf{v}_i$. We can prove that these $\mathbf{u}_i$ vectors are orthonormal: $\mathbf{u}_i^{\top} \mathbf{u}_j = \frac{1}{\sigma_i \sigma_j} \mathbf{v}_i^{\top} \mathbf{A}^{\top} \mathbf{A} \mathbf{v}_j = \frac{\lambda_j}{\sigma_i \sigma_j} \mathbf{v}_i^{\top} \mathbf{v}_j$. If $i = j$, this equals 1; if $i \neq j$, this equals 0. Stacking these equations yields $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}$, which leads directly to $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^{\top}$.

callouts.title: GPU Acceleration of SVD in Production
callouts.content: In deep learning frameworks, SVD is calculated iteratively. For small or medium matrices, libraries like NVIDIA cuSOLVER implement block Jacobi methods which are parallelized across GPU warps. For large sparse matrices, randomized SVD is used, projecting the matrix onto a low-dimensional subspace using random projection matrices to achieve a 10x compute reduction.

content_sections.title: Low-Rank Approximation, Collaborative Filtering, and LSA
content_sections.content:
In many machine learning applications, matrices are extremely large but contain highly redundant information. The rank $r$ of a matrix $\mathbf{A}$ represents the number of independent rows or columns. A low-rank approximation seeks to replace $\mathbf{A}$ with a matrix $\mathbf{A}_k$ of rank $k < r$ that minimizes the reconstruction error. The Eckart-Young-Mirsky Theorem proves that the optimal rank-$k$ approximation of $\mathbf{A}$ under both the Frobenius norm and spectral norm is obtained by truncating the SVD of $\mathbf{A}$ after the first $k$ terms:

$$\mathbf{A}_k = \mathbf{U}_k \mathbf{\Sigma}_k \mathbf{V}_k^{\top} = \sum_{i=1}^k \sigma_i \mathbf{u}_i \mathbf{v}_i^{\top}$$

where $\mathbf{U}_k$ and $\mathbf{V}_k$ contain the first $k$ columns of $\mathbf{U}$ and $\mathbf{V}$, and $\mathbf{\Sigma}_k$ contains the first $k$ singular values. The squared Frobenius norm of the approximation error is exactly equal to the sum of the squares of the discarded singular values: $\sum_{i=k+1}^r \sigma_i^2$.

In Collaborative Filtering, the user-item interaction matrix $\mathbf{R}$ is highly sparse and high-dimensional. Truncating the SVD of $\mathbf{R}$ to a low rank $k$ maps both users and items into a shared latent space of dimension $k$. The rating prediction for user $i$ and item $j$ is computed as the dot product of their latent vectors: $R_{ij} = \mathbf{u}_i^{\top} \mathbf{v}_j$. Similarly, in Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA), applying SVD to a term-document matrix projects words and documents into a semantic space. This groups synonyms together along the principal singular vectors, enabling concept-based search retrieval.

callouts.title: Center Your Data Before SVD and PCA
callouts.content: SVD on an uncentered matrix extracts axes that point toward the global mean of the data rather than the directions of maximum variance. When implementing PCA or low-rank dimensionality reduction, always subtract the mean of each feature first. Without centering, the first singular vector will simply align with the dataset's center of mass, distorting the principal components.

content_sections.title: Professional Practice and Interview Insights
content_sections.content:
In mathematical software interviews, engineers are frequently asked to prove properties of singular values and show how SVD is applied to recommendation algorithms. A classic question concerns how SVD behaves under missing data. In real-world recommendation systems, over 99% of the rating matrix $\mathbf{R}$ is empty. Standard SVD is mathematically undefined for matrices with missing values. Imputing these values with zeros or column means introduces major bias. In production systems, engineers bypass this by using regularized matrix factorization algorithms (like FunkSVD or Alternating Least Squares) that minimize the loss function strictly over the observed ratings:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{(i,j) \in \Omega} (R_{ij} - \mathbf{u}_i^{\top} \mathbf{v}_j)^2 + \lambda (\|\mathbf{u}_i\|_2^2 + \|\mathbf{v}_j\|_2^2)$$

where $\Omega$ is the set of observed rating indices. This optimization approach avoids computing SVD altogether, using gradient descent to find the factorized matrices directly.

callouts.title: SVD vs Eigendecomposition Dimensions
callouts.content: Interviewers at top-tier firms will ask: 'Why do we prefer SVD over Eigendecomposition for collaborative filtering?' The correct response has two parts: First, the user-item matrix is rectangular, which instantly disqualifies Eigendecomposition. Second, even if we construct the square user covariance matrix, SVD provides orthonormal bases for both the user space and the item space simultaneously via the left- and right-singular vectors.
